data.title: SA Asks: What's the best SMID AI stock right now?
data.description:
What's the best small or mid-cap stock in the AI space right now for investors?

Seeking Alpha analysts Bay Area Ideas, Yiannis Zourmpanos, Michael Del Monte, and Julian Lin  weigh in.

Bay Area Ideas: Among SMID AI stocks, Applied Digital (NASDAQ:APLD) is a standout. Their largest growth driver is HPC hosting, and with hyperscaler demand for compute being unrelenting, they've been a major beneficiary. In FY2026 Q1, revenue growth rocketed from 41% in the previous quarter to 84%, so demand is accelerating. For their bottom line, they've been able to narrow their per-share losses year-over-year from $0.15 to just $0.03. They're on the verge of turning profitable.  

Looking forward, Polaris Forge 1 is set to open before the end of the calendar year, while two other facilities are in the works for launches in 2026 and 2027. With compute demand being robust among hyperscalers, this increased capacity will fuel Applied Digital's (NASDAQ:APLD) growth.  

While the stock is trading at an elevated forward P/S ratio of 31.38, with the company targeting an NOI run rate of $1 billion within 5 years, the valuation isn't as expensive as it seems. The company currently has a market cap of ~$8 billion, so if they hit their targets, the stock may actually be cheap. Given the opportunity ahead and the not-so-outrageous valuation, Applied Digital (NASDAQ:APLD) stock is well positioned to outperform.  

Yiannis Zourmpanos: Here are my top picks right now. 

Eos Energy Enterprises (NASDAQ:EOSE) sits at the crossroads of the AI power boom and America’s energy independence movement. While lithium-ion incumbents face fire risk and China exposure, Eos is scaling a domestic, non-flammable zinc-bromine platform backed by over $90 million per year in 45X production credits per line and a $19 billion project pipeline feeding hyperscaler and grid-scale demand. 

CleanSpark (NASDAQ:CLSK) stands at the intersection of Bitcoin and AI-driven energy infrastructure. While IREN (NASDAQ:IREN) had a strong bull run and chased GPU headlines, CleanSpark quietly commands 1+ GW of U.S. power, 50 EH/s of hash rate, and 55% margins, all self-funded and primed to evolve into America’s first true digital compute utility. 

Michael Del Monte: Regal Rexnord (NYSE:RRX) is an appealing name in the SMID space, operating across markets that can drive secular growth, including reindustrialization, industrial automation, and data center buildouts. The company manufactures various parts and components, as well as complete systems utilized across these markets, including electric motors, bearings, switchgear, and automation systems for manufacturing processes. The stock is trading at a relatively low premium of 10.66x EV/aEBITDA, a modest discount to its historical midpoint of roughly 12.25x. The company has been deleveraging its balance sheet in recent quarters, freeing up capital for organic growth. 

Julian Lin: I urge caution on chasing AI names, not just due to valuation, but also given the potential disruption of which the full ramifications have not been revealed. 

I prefer to instead focus on the large-cap plays, including Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL). These might not offer the same explosive upside, but that is compensated by their high cash flow generation as well as their “picks and shovels” positioning in cloud computing. I see solid upside for both names ahead.

  • Trending AI Stocks

data.title: Astra, GSK up after report on U.K. – U.S. drug pricing deal
data.description:
• Leading U.K.-based drugmakers AstraZeneca (NASDAQ:AZN) and GSK (NYSE:GSK) traded higher on Friday as The Financial Times reported that the U.K. government believes it is close to signing a major drug pricing deal with the Trump administration.
  • As part of the deal, Britain is expected to pay billions of pounds additionally to procure some of the drugs manufactured in the U.S. In return, the White House will impose “low to zero” tariffs on U.K. pharma imports.
  • “We are quite close,” said one official of Sir Keir Starmer’s government. “There have been extensive talks over the last 10 days.
  • “The pharma companies are pushing for a better deal, but we’ve told the U.S. this is as far as we can go. We are demonstrating that we are taking on board their concerns.”

data.title: OpenAI's ChatGPT mobile app seeing slowing download growth, daily use: report
data.description:
OpenAI's (OPENAI) ChatGPT mobile app growth may have hit its peak, TechCrunch reported, citing a new analysis of download trends and daily active users provided by Apptopia.

Its estimates suggest that new user growth, measured by percentage changes in new global downloads, slowed after April. Global daily active user growth also stopped and plateaued in more recent weeks, the report added.

OpenAI did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

The firm analyzed global daily active user, or DAU, growth and found that the numbers have started to even out over the past month or so, according to the report.

October is only half over, but the firm noted that it is on track to decline 8.1% in terms of a month-over-month percentage change in global downloads, the report added.

The report noted that the analysis was about download growth not total downloads. In terms of number of new installs, ChatGPT’s mobile app is still doing well, with millions of downloads per day, the report added.

Other metrics suggest that average time spent per DAU in the U.S.,  has declined 22.5% since July, and average sessions per DAU in the U.S. have also fallen by 20.7%.

This suggests that U.S. users are spending less time in ChatGPT’s app and are opening it fewer times per day. User churn in the U.S. has also declined and stabilized during this time, suggesting that the app is now retaining its core users and seeing fewer who just drop in briefly to experiment, then leave the app, the report noted.

Besides simply reaching its peak, there could be other factors that may have caused this. This includes competition from Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) unit Google’s Gemini, and user engagement changes following an April update that was aimed at making the chatbot’s AI model less sycophantic. This continued with the August release of GPT-5, which was said to be less personable, the report added.

Apptopia noted that ChatGPT’s average time spent per DAU and average sessions per DAU metrics were trending lower before the sharp rise of its rival Gemini, which raced to the top of the charts in September thanks to the release of Google’s new AI image model, Nano Banana, the report noted.

Gemini’s growth could have influenced some of the more recent decline in ChatGPT’s core metrics, but it does not explain the overall trend, according to the report.

In addition, Apptopia noted that if only average time spent per DAU was declining, but not average sessions per DAU, it could have suggested that people were getting more efficient with their ChatGPT queries. But since both are on the decline, that is not the case.

Apptopia said that it is possible that the experimentation phase with the ChatGPT app is over, and now it is becoming a part of users’ daily routines. People are likely using the app when they need it or remember to use it, versus the increased use it saw when it was still new, the report added.

data.title: Huntington raises 2025 guidance to 11% NII growth as Veritex acquisition accelerates Texas expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Huntington Bancshares Incorporated (HBAN) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Stephen Steinour, Chairman, President & CEO, emphasized, "We delivered another outstanding quarter. The business is performing exceptionally well across all fronts. We have tremendous momentum, and we are poised to accelerate from here." He highlighted top-tier profitability, outperformance in loan and deposit growth compared to peers, and strong positive operating leverage.
  • Steinour announced, "We are poised to further accelerate our growth in Texas. We look forward to welcoming our Veritex colleagues and customers to Huntington next Monday...we will achieve immediate scale in Texas...the 14th largest depository in the state and the fifth largest in Dallas."
  • Steinour detailed expectations to execute on cost synergies from the Veritex acquisition, projecting a 1 percentage point improvement in efficiency ratio and approximately 30 basis points lift to ROTCE, with greater opportunity anticipated from revenue growth synergies.
  • Zachary Wasserman, CFO & Senior EVP, stated, "Huntington delivered another outstanding quarter with earnings per common share of $0.41...adjusted EPS was $0.40, up 18% year-over-year. Average loan balances grew by $2.8 billion or 2% from the prior quarter, while average deposits increased by $1.4 billion or 1%. Reported CET1 ended the quarter at 10.6% with adjusted CET1 at 9.2%."

Outlook

  • Wasserman reported, "We are again raising our financial guidance for the year. On a Huntington stand-alone basis, we are increasing our net interest income full year guidance by 2 percentage points to 10% to 11% from the prior range of 8% to 9%."
  • Inclusive of Veritex, full year average deposit balance (ADB) growth is projected at approximately 6.5% to 7%. Stand-alone loan growth is expected at the high end of the guidance range at approximately 8%, with a combined guidance of 9% to 9.5% including Veritex.
  • Full year fee income guidance increased to approximately 7% from the prior 4% to 6%. Operating leverage outlook for 2025 is now more than 2.5 percentage points of efficiency ratio improvement.
  • For Q4, Wasserman forecasted approximately $20 million of core PPNR benefit from Veritex and $0.01 EPS contribution, with acquisition-related expenses of approximately $125 million to $150 million expected at closing or shortly thereafter.

Financial Results

  • Huntington reported earnings per common share of $0.41. Adjusted EPS, excluding certain items, was $0.40, up 18% year-over-year.
  • Average loan balances increased by $2.8 billion or 2% from the prior quarter; average deposits rose by $1.4 billion or 1%.
  • Net interest income grew approximately $40 million or 2.7% sequentially, representing nearly 12% year-over-year growth. Net interest margin for the third quarter was 3.13%.
  • Adjusted PPNR expanded 16% year-over-year, tangible book value grew by 10%, and adjusted ROTCE was above 17%.
  • Payments, Wealth Management, and Capital Markets collectively grew 13% year-over-year in fee businesses.
  • Noninterest expense was $1.2 billion, with a focus on cost efficiency and investment to drive positive operating leverage. On a trailing 12-month adjusted basis, 500 basis points of positive operating leverage was generated.

Q&A

  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC Capital Markets: Questioned loan growth outlook. Wasserman responded that "confidence is high for the fourth quarter...our working assumption is somewhere in the mid- to high single digits for year-over-year loan growth in 2026 as well."
  • Arfstrom asked about credit quality. Steinour replied, "We've had a really exceptional year in terms of credit performance, and our outlook would suggest that's going to continue...We're not seeing anything at this point that concerns us."
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about safeguards against fraud and NDFI book risks. Brendan Lawlor, Chief Credit Officer, explained the company's "aggregate moderate to low-risk appetite" and active portfolio management, noting NDFI exposure is "right around 2% of total loans."
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: Asked about operating leverage. Wasserman said, "The approach we're taking is very sustainable...continuing to drive fundamental reengineering into the cost base...and it's our approach for the next several years."
  • McGratty queried about M&A. Steinour emphasized, "Our focus is driving the organic growth of the company. We were not involved in Comerica."
  • Nathan Stein, Deutsche Bank: Sought details on NII outlook for 2026. Wasserman confirmed expectations for "mid- to high single digits in loan growth" and "at least 10 basis points of spread revenue expansion."
  • Steven Alexopoulos, TD Cowen: Asked about industry credit quality. Steinour stated, "I think the industry has derisked since the GFC...I think the industry is in very good shape."
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA Securities: Inquired about deposit competition and loan growth. Wasserman stated, "We're seeing very strong marginal returns...it's a battle of sort of 100 different levers all at the same time."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts demonstrated a slightly positive tone, focusing on operational momentum and competitive positioning while probing on credit quality, NIM sustainability, and integration of Veritex.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly expressing optimism about growth, credit quality, and execution, as seen in Steinour's closing: "We've never been better positioned, and we're very confident in our ability to drive continued strong performance."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence increased, particularly regarding the Veritex integration and upward-revised financial guidance. Analyst sentiment remained constructive but included deeper questioning around risk and sustainability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Net interest income growth guidance raised to 10% to 11% from 8% to 9% previously, reflecting stronger-than-expected loan growth and margin expansion.
  • Fee income growth guidance increased to 7% from a prior range of 4% to 6%.
  • Loan and deposit growth guidance both increased, with the inclusion of Veritex accelerating projections.
  • Management's tone was more assertive this quarter, highlighting integration certainty and expanded market share in Texas, compared to a more measured optimism last quarter.
  • Analysts maintained focus on core operational drivers and integration risk, with slightly more emphasis on sustainable profitability and credit controls.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management addressed industry credit risk concerns, emphasizing the company's "aggregate moderate to low-risk appetite" and robust portfolio management.
  • Lawlor cited active monitoring and relationship-based lending as safeguards against recent industry fraud events, noting the company's NDFI exposure is well diversified and minimal.
  • Management stated, "We're not seeing anything at this point that concerns us" regarding credit quality, and highlighted ongoing expense discipline and operational efficiencies to manage potential headwinds.

Final Takeaway

Huntington Bancshares emphasized a period of exceptional operational performance, raising its 2025 guidance on both net interest income and fee growth while delivering peer-leading loan and deposit expansion. The Veritex acquisition is positioned as a springboard for accelerated Texas growth, with management expressing confidence in seamless integration and realization of both cost and revenue synergies. Credit quality remains strong, underpinned by disciplined risk management, and the balance sheet is described as well-capitalized. With tangible book value and ROTCE both advancing, management maintains high confidence in delivering continued strong results and long-term shareholder value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Charles Schwab sees divergent investor behavior: retail buys the dip, institutions hedge risk
data.description:
Charles Schwab’s head trading strategist Joe Mazzola highlighted a notable divergence between institutional and retail investor behavior in the current market environment.

While institutions are actively hedging against downside risks, retail investors continue to show optimism, particularly in technology stocks (VGT), (XLK).

“Equity call volume…tends to be more of a retail type trade,” he said, suggesting retail investors still anticipate further market upside.

According to the strategist, retail investors have been directing capital toward several key sectors.

“There’s been some inflows in the energy (XLE), communication services (XLC), discretionary (XLY),” he noted, adding that healthcare (XLV) has also attracted investor attention. He also said that Schwab clients typically engage in “buying on the dips” for major stocks like Tesla (TSLA) and Amazon (AMZN).

The upcoming earnings season represents a critical juncture for market sentiment, particularly as it will be the first to fully reflect the impact of recent tariffs. Mazzola pointed out that unlike previous quarters, analysts have actually raised their estimates heading into Q3 reporting.

“Expectations are for about 9% year-over-year growth,” he said.

Lastly, market uncertainty is being amplified by the current lack of government economic data that typically provides context ahead of earnings reports.

“That’s causing a little bit of the skittishness in the market right now because they don’t have that macro backdrop,” Mazzola said, concluding that continued market momentum will largely depend on companies delivering strong quarterly results.

data.title: Fed's Musalem open to another rate cut if further risks to job market emerge
data.description:
Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis President Alberto Musalem said Friday the U.S. central bank could consider another interest-rate cut if the labor market continues to weaken, while underscoring the need for a flexible, data-driven approach to monetary policy, with inflation still elevated.

“I could support a path with an additional reduction in the policy rate if there are further risks to the labor market that emerge,” Musalem said at an event in Washington, adding that further easing would also hinge on inflation staying in check.

“I do think we need to not be on a preset course,” he said, amid economic uncertainty. “Right now, I think it’s particularly important to go meeting by meeting.”

Last week, the St. Louis Fed chief said he would consider future rate reductions "to provide further assurance against labor-market weakening," while emphasizing the need to "tread with caution, because there's limited room for easing before monetary policy can become overly accommodative."

data.title: Truist targets 15% ROTCE by 2027 as revenue growth and buybacks accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Truist Financial Corporation (TFC) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO William Rogers highlighted a strategic investment in building 100 new branches in high-growth markets, renovating more than 300 locations, and enhancing digital capabilities. Rogers stated, "These new branches are designed for smarter client engagement with advanced AI-driven technology and dedicated premier adviser spaces all aimed at helping clients achieve financial success and further strengthening our presence in these dynamic communities."
  • Rogers reported, "For the third quarter, we reported net income available to common shareholders of $1.3 billion or $1.04 a share which included $0.02 a share of restructuring charges primarily related to severance." He emphasized the company's strong revenue performance, disciplined expense and credit management, and robust capital return, noting a 130 basis points sequential improvement in ROTCE to 13.6%.
  • Rogers described momentum across Consumer and Small Business Banking, with over 20,000 net new checking accounts and growth in balances, and stated, "Gen Z and Millennials represented 63% of this growth, a strong signal that our digital-first approach is resonating with the next generation of Truist clients."
  • Rogers said, "In Wholesale, I'm encouraged by this quarter's loan growth, improvement in investment banking and trading revenue and progress in key focus areas like payments and wealth."
  • CFO Michael Maguire added, "Adjusted revenue increased 3.7% linked quarter due to 9.9% growth in noninterest income and 1.2% growth in net interest income. Adjusted expenses increased 1% linked quarter, primarily due to higher personnel expenses related to incentives and strategic hiring efforts."

Outlook

  • Maguire stated, "Looking into the fourth quarter of 2025, we expect revenue to increase by approximately 1% to 2% relative to third quarter revenue of $5.2 billion. We expect net interest income to increase approximately 2% in the fourth quarter primarily driven by loan growth and lower deposit costs."
  • For full year 2025, management maintained its outlook for revenue growth of 1.5% to 2.5% and adjusted expense growth of approximately 1%. Maguire indicated, "We expect our effective tax rate to approximate 17.5% or 20% on a taxable equivalent basis in 2025."
  • Rogers projected, "We expect the rate of revenue growth in 2026 to more than double versus our growth rate this year. We also expect to generate more operating leverage in 2026 than we'll generate this year."
  • Rogers confirmed, "We're targeting a 15% ROTCE in 2027."

Financial Results

  • Net income available to common shareholders was $1.3 billion, or $1.04 per share, including $0.02 per share of restructuring charges.
  • Adjusted noninterest income increased 9.9% linked quarter to over $1.5 billion, driven by investment banking, trading, and wealth management income.
  • Average loan balances grew 2.5% linked quarter to $320 billion. Commercial loans saw a $4.8 billion, or 2.6%, increase.
  • Average deposits decreased $3.9 billion sequentially, primarily due to the withdrawal of $10.9 billion of short-term M&A-related deposits.
  • Asset quality metrics remained solid, with net charge-offs declining to 48 basis points and the ALLL ratio steady at 1.54% of total loans.
  • The company returned $1.2 billion of capital to shareholders through dividends and repurchased $500 million of common stock, with plans to target $750 million in share repurchases during the fourth quarter.

Q&A

  • John Pancari, Evercore: Asked for details on revenue growth components for 2026 and operating leverage. Rogers replied, "NII growth is a key part of our revenue... we'll have both loan and deposit growth, deposit growth better than this year and loan growth, probably a little more consistent."
  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: Inquired about deposit pricing and buyback flexibility. Maguire answered, "We saw a touch of momentum in the third quarter... we thought we could get to sort of the mid-40s (beta) and that takes into consideration some of the momentum that we are seeing now."
  • Erika Penala, UBS: Questioned efficiency ratio targets and balancing investment. Rogers explained efficiency ratio will see marginal improvement, but "we're going to continue to invest... we're going to be driven by growth."
  • Kenneth Usdin, Bernstein: Asked about fixed rate loan repricing. Rogers responded, "That's going to be, I think, a nice tailwind for us in '26, but a diminishing tailwind with perhaps a long tail."
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA: Sought a credit quality health check. Chief Risk Officer Brad Bender said, "The majority of our consumer portfolios are high-quality super-prime borrowers. We derisked in '23 and '24 on the lower end of the consumer spectrum."
  • Chris McGratty, KBW: Asked if First Brands exposure is reserved for. Bender confirmed, "We've got it accounted for in the forward guide."
  • Steven Alexopoulos, TD Cowen: Pressed on NDFI portfolio risk. Bender replied, "Today, we sit at about 11% of our total lending portfolio... well diversified, granular and then underweight relative."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone reflected cautious optimism, with recurring questions on deposit growth, loan pricing, capital return, credit quality, and NDFI exposures. Several questions pressed for greater detail on 2026 guidance and risk management amid industry events.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently citing "momentum" and "confidence" in strategic initiatives. Rogers repeatedly referenced being "optimistic" and "focused on executing with discipline."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased focus on forward-looking revenue acceleration, operating leverage, and risk exposures, especially regarding NDFI and credit issues.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for revenue and expense growth in 2025 remained unchanged; however, management provided substantially more detail on anticipated revenue acceleration and ROTCE targets for 2026 and 2027 in the current call.
  • Current quarter saw a shift in strategic focus to AI-driven branch investments and further digital engagement, while credit quality and risk diversification remained recurring analyst concerns.
  • Key metrics—like net income, loan growth, and noninterest income—showed sequential improvement compared to Q2, with greater emphasis on shareholder returns through expanded buyback plans.
  • Management’s tone was more assertively optimistic about achieving operating leverage and ROTCE targets than in the prior quarter.
  • Analysts’ tone grew slightly more skeptical, repeatedly questioning risk exposures.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged continued vigilance on credit risk, particularly NDFI exposures and recent market events. Rogers said, “The #1 risk for a bank is credit risk... we have been in the past. We're hypervigilant today and will be hypervigilant tomorrow.”
  • Bender confirmed ongoing monitoring of First Brands exposure, noting it is fully reserved and incorporated into guidance.
  • Potential for diminishing tailwinds from fixed rate loan repricing was discussed, with management indicating ongoing benefit but at a reduced pace.
  • Analyst concerns centered on deposit trends, loan growth sustainability, and how AI and digital investments will manifest in financial results.

Final Takeaway

Truist management underscored solid third quarter performance and announced aggressive investments in digital and branch expansion, with a clear pathway outlined to achieving a 15% ROTCE in 2027. Momentum in loan and deposit growth, digital engagement, and wealth management, combined with disciplined cost control and robust capital return, support confidence in stronger revenue growth and profitability targets for 2026 and beyond. Management remains vigilant on credit risks while emphasizing strategic investments and shareholder value creation as central to their long-term outlook.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wall Street searches for direction as investors navigate worries on regional banks' health
data.description:
U.S. stocks seesawed on Friday, as Wall Street was rattled by concerns on the health of regional banks and credit risks while the U.S. government shutdown entered its 17th day.

The major indexes wavered throughout Friday's session. The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.1%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was flat, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.2%.

“Markets are now paying attention to credit, and there are likely more skeletons in the closet of lenders. I am not sure yet whether this poses systemic risk, but the gaping jaw between business development companies and the S&P 500 will close one way or another,” said Chester Ntonifor, chief strategist at BCA Research, via X.com.

Stocks of investment bank Jefferies Financial (JEF) +4.2%, regional banks Zions Bancorporation (ZION) +4.1%, and Western Alliance (WAL) +1.8% were initially lower on Friday as investors worried about the quality of loans and investments made by the firms, but have since recovered. Goldman Sachs (GS) and J.P. Morgan (JPM), however, are some of the top decliners in the Dow (DJI).

The KBW Nasdaq Regional Banking Index (KRX) closed down 6.3% on Thursday trading, its biggest drop since April, following the disclosures by two banks of irregularities related to some loans -- last +1.1%.

Meanwhile, the U.S. government shutdown continued to grind on as it entered its third week, after a GOP funding bill failed for the 10th time. Prediction market Kalshi now sees the government shutdown lasting almost 40 days, the longest in history.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 4 basis points to 4.01%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 3 basis points to 3.46%.

“The bond market is pricing in 2 more 25 bps rate cuts by year-end and 3 more 25 bps cuts in 2026. That would bring the Fed Funds Rate below 3%. After a brief hiatus, easy money is back,” said Charlie Bilello, chief market strategist at Creative Planning.

Wall Street's major averages closed lower on Thursday amid increased volatility due to the ongoing U.S.-China trade conflict and bad loan reports in banks, while investors continued to assess earnings reports and the ongoing U.S. government shutdown.

data.title: Progyny seesaws amid confusion over impact from Trump’s IVF initiative
data.description:
Shares of fertility benefits provider Progyny (NASDAQ:PGNY) turned volatile as the Trump administration announced a new initiative to expand access to in vitro fertilization alongside a major drug pricing deal with fertility treatment maker EMD Serono of Merck KGaA (OTCPK:MKGAF).

Progyny (NASDAQ:PGNY) gained sharply before closing ~5% higher on Thursday as the New York Times reported about the upcoming announcement ahead of its launch by President Donald Trump at the White House later that day.

The stock fell ~10% on Friday as Wall Street analysts weighed in on EMD Serono’s deal with the Trump administration, under which the company has agreed to offer its IVF therapies at steep discounts to cash-paying patients through the government’s direct-to-consumer site, TrumpRx.

“The volatility in PGNY shares, including the move higher intraday on Thursday and the pre-market weakness Friday, shows the confusion about how to read this fertility push for PGNY,” Leerink analyst Michael Cherny wrote, according to Bloomberg News.

As part of the agreement, Merck KGaA (MKGAY) became the third leading drugmaker after Pfizer (PFE) and AstraZeneca (AZN) to lower U.S. prices of their products in line with those in other developed countries under Trump’s most-favored-nation drug pricing policy.

Cherny, with an Outperform rating on Progyny (NASDAQ:PGNY), argued that “popular feedback has been negative, with concerns that the lower cash-pay price will erode PGNY’s Rx revenue, that is roughly 1/3 of company with similar gross margins.”

Additionally, analysts noted the administration’s efforts to establish a new benefit option through which employers will be able to deliver fertility benefits directly to employees as part of standalone benefit packages.

Leerink said it is not yet convinced how the initiative will impact PGNY’s value proposition, but added that “the support of carve-out benefits as more likely to benefit PGNY LT than disrupt the model.”

Truist analyst Jailendra Singh argued that expansion of fertility benefits among small and midsize employers is “broadly viewed as a net positive for Progyny (and the broader fertility industry) as this direction aligns with Progyny’s existing roadmap.”

The company “emphasizes that “Most Favored Nation” and TrumpRx pricing initiatives apply only to cash-pay patients, specifically underinsured or uninsured individuals,” Singh wrote with a Hold rating on PGNY.

data.title: Deutsche Bank loan book stays sound after First Brands, Tricolor blowups, CEO says
data.description:
At a time when confidence across the credit space is being tested after recent blowups, Deutsche Bank (NYSE:DB) CEO Christian Sewing said Friday the German lender has seen no signs of credit deterioration in its loan book.

“There is no deterioration, we’re very confident with our credit portfolio,” Deutsche Bank CEO Christian Sewing said in an interview on Bloomberg Television Friday.

The recent failures of auto lender Tricolor Holdings and First Brands Group -- the auto-parts supplier with over $10B in liabilities have left some major Wall Street creditors exposed, including Jefferies Financial Group (JEF) and UBS Group (UBS).

JPMorgan Chase (JPM), took a $170M charge-off related to its Tricolor exposure, which CEO Jamie Dimon publicly called "not our finest moment" and also used a waterbug reference. "When you see one cockroach, there are probably more," he declared, before many more banks revealed additional fallout.

DB shares slid 4.3% in afternoon trading in the U.S.

Meanwhile, U.S. bank stocks started the climb back up after suffering a jolt down on Thursday, as bank commentary and analysts pointed out that banks' fundamentals appear to be generally solid, and the instances of credit risk events aren't representative of the broader sector.
